[[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = Nukisaki Haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|18.55|N|138|51|27.55|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Futsunushi | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = Nukisaki Haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|18.55|N|138|51|27.55|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Futsunushi | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

|first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = Nukisaki Haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|18.55|N|138|51|27.55|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Futsunushi | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

[[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = Nukisaki Haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|18.55|N|138|51|27.55|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Futsunushi | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = Nukisaki Haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|18.55|N|138|51|27.55|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Futsunushi | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]]

The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = Nukisaki Haiden.JPG | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|18.55|N|138|51|27.55|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Futsunushi | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tomioka, Gunma| Tomioka]] in [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumomoto]] Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a [[:en:Fluvial terrace|Fluvial terrace]] on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]], facing the main route to [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano]]. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] [[sn:ukisaki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]]

Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society.
