[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10863796}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja]][[en:Nukisaki Shrine]][[ja:一之宮貫前神社]][[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja]][[en:Nukisaki Shrine]][[ja:一之宮貫前神社]][[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja]][[en:Nukisaki Shrine]][[ja:一之宮貫前神社]][[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10863796]] Wikidata name: Ichinomiya Nukisaki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = File:Ichinomiya Nukisaki-jinja, shaden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.255153|N|138.857653|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}; {{ill|Himegami|WD=Q22070227}} | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10863796}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja]][[en:Nukisaki Shrine]][[ja:一之宮貫前神社]][[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja]][[en:Nukisaki Shrine]][[ja:一之宮貫前神社]][[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja]][[en:Nukisaki Shrine]][[ja:一之宮貫前神社]][[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10863796]] Wikidata name: Ichinomiya Nukisaki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Nukisaki Jinja
貫前神社 | image = File:Ichinomiya Nukisaki-jinja, shaden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.255153|N|138.857653|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286}}; {{ill|Himegami|WD=Q22070227}} | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10863796}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10863796}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]

[[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10863796}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]

[[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Cupronickel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Tang Dynasty, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} *[http://www.nukisaki.or.jp Official home page] *[https://www.city.tomioka.lg.jp/www/contents/1000000000981/index.html Tomioka City officialdom page] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Nukisaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Ichinomiya nukisaki-jinja}} [[ja:一之宮貫前神社]] [[zh:一之宮貫前神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10863796}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Second Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]

[[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835|1=Tomioka, Gunma}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka|1=Tomioka, Gunma}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace|1=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|1=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|1=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976.{{cite web |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1917.{{cite web 貫前神社 | image = File:Ichinomiya Nukisaki-jinja, shaden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.255153|N|138.857653|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|1=Himegami}} | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835|1=Tomioka, Gunma}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka|1=Tomioka, Gunma}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace|1=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|1=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|1=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/413|title=貫前神社〈楼門〉|trans-title=Rōmon|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See 貫前神社 | image = File:Ichinomiya Nukisaki-jinja, shaden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Nukisaki Jinja (ICP) {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.255153|N|138.857653|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Futsunushi|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}; {{ill|Himegami|WD=Q22070227|1=Himegami}} | founded_by = c. [[Emperor Akan]] | established = c.534 | date_destroyed = | location =1535 Ichinomiya, Tomioka-shi, Gunma-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.nukisaki.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = March 15 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tomioka|WD=Q873835|1=Tomioka, Gunma}} }} {{nihongo|'''Nukisaki Jinja'''|貫前神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt= Tomioka|1=Tomioka, Gunma}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} It is one of only three shrines in all of Japan where visitors enter from the top and descend downwards into the shrine; the other two are [[Udo Jingū|Udo Shrine]] and Kusakabe Yoshimi Shrine in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumomoto|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} Prefectures respectively.{{cite web |title=全国的にも珍しい？1500th year の時を刻む「一之宮貫前神社」（いちのみやぬきさきじんじゃ）｜ふるさと納税完全ガイド｜ふるさぽ |url=https://furusapo.fururi.jp/article/gunma-tomioka-ichinomiyasakijinja/ |website=ふるさと納税ガイド「ふるさぽ」｜はじめてでもわかりやすい組みと手続き |access-date=27 June 2022 |language=ja |date=29 August 2017}} Nukisaki Shrine is also featured on the 'yu' card in [[:en:Jomo Karuta|Jomo Karuta]]. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Nukisaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Futsunushi| Futsunushi-no-kami]]'''| 経津主神 |}}, god of war and tutelary deity of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Fujiwara clan]]s * {{nihongo|'''Hime-Ōkami'''| 姫大神|}}, goddess of sericulture ==History== The Nukisaki Jinja is located on a {{ill|Fluvial terrace|en|Fluvial terrace|WD=Q1069618|lt=Fluvial terrace|1=Fluvial terrace}} on the left bank of the Kabura River in southwestern {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, facing the main route to {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano|1=Shinano Province}}. The origins of Nukisaki Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, the shrine clams that it was founded in the first year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Ankan]], or in 534. The shrine first appears in the historical record in 806, and its first mention in national records is an entry in the "[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]" in 839, followed by the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in 859. In the 927 ''[[Engishiki]]'' records it is listed as a {{nihongo|myojin taisha|名神大社||}}. Emperor Uda decided to send an imperial messenger to the shrine for a once-in-a-lifetime memorial service in 888, but this did not take-lace until the enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} in 1017. It was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province from around 1100.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and afterwards, the shrine was patronized by the warrior class, including the [[Minamoto clan]], [[Uesugi clan]], {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} and [[Takeda clan]], with the shrine times acting as an intermediary between the warring parties, and receiving donations of estates and for rebuilding in return. The current shrine structures were largely constructed under {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} and [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|en|Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q11358969|lt=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station|1=Jōshū-Ichinomiya Station}} on the {{ill|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|en|Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|WD=Q846501|lt=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line|1=Jōshin Dentetsu Jōshin Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Nukisaki Honden.JPG|Honden (ICP) Nukisaki Komado.JPG|Raijin Komado on the Honden Nukisaki Haiden Saishiki.JPG|Decorations on the Honden 一之宮貫前神社 楼門.JPG|Romon (ICP) 一之宮貫前神社 不明門・勅額鳥居.JPG|Imperial Messenger Gate ==Cultural Properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== * {{nihongo|'''[[Honden]]'''|本殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/411|title=貫前神社〈本殿〉|trans-title=Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'''|拝殿|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/412|title=貫前神社〈拝殿〉|trans-title=Haiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'''|楼門|}}, Edo period (1635), designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1976.{{cite web Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1912.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5187|title=白銅月宮鑑|trans-title=Hakudōgekkyōkan|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} * {{nihongo|'''Bronzel Mirror'''|白銅月宮鑑|}}, Kamakura period, set of 2, designated a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1917.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5188|title=銅鏡｛〈梅雀文様一／竹虎文様一〉｝|trans-title=dōkyō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See
